behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is module handoff (file discovery/parsing/sorting -> execution loop), not a single pure function. A unit test of only the sort helper can miss bugs where the runner applies in discovery order instead of sorted order. Rejected higher level: System is disqualified because behavior text only requires correct ordering logic, not real subprocess/OS/network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp migrations directory with out-of-order filenames (for example 010_b.sql, 002_a.sql, 100_c.sql), run the runner with a test DB/repo adapter, and assert executed migration sequence is 002, 010, 100 and metadata records reflect that same order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is idempotency across runs via persisted metadata state, so it depends on interaction between runner logic and migration-state storage. Unit is too low because it can mock state transitions that differ from real storage behavior. Rejected higher level: System is disqualified because behavior text does not require real OS/subprocess conditions, only correct persisted-state checks between runs.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner once with a migration that has an observable side effect, then run it again against the same test database. Assert the migration SQL executes only once and _migrations contains one record for that version.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: The failure is transactional coordination across runner + DB transaction boundary + migration ledger updates. A unit fake often hides real rollback semantics and partial-write edge cases. Rejected higher level: System is disqualified because the behavior is about transactional correctness, not OS/subprocess/network timing effects.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare migration N that writes data then raises an error, followed by migration N+1 with a distinct side effect. Execute runner and assert: data from N is absent (rolled back), N is not marked applied, N+1 was never executed, and run exits with failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: This explicitly requires concurrent separate processes. Real failure modes (race windows, lock acquisition timing, duplicate execution) appear at process/DB concurrency boundaries and are frequently hidden by in-process mocks. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by behavior text 'two instances ... at the same time' and 'across both instances', which requires real concurrent runtime behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start two runner processes simultaneously against the same test PostgreSQL instance and the same migrations directory. After both finish, assert each migration's side effects appear exactly once and _migrations has one row per version with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is startup orchestration/order-of-operations across schema bootstrap and migration execution modules. Unit is too low because function-level checks can miss real initialization sequencing with storage. Rejected higher level: System is disqualified because no real subprocess/OS behavior is required; local module wiring with a test DB is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fresh empty test database and a first migration that depends on _migrations bootstrap ordering (or record precise execution events). Run once and assert _migrations exists before first migration apply step, and the run completes with expected applied-version records.
